title: Algorithm to Automatically Adjust Repeater Parameters to Optimize to the Local Mesh Density

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Attached is a document that examines the various repeater parameters (rxdelay, txdelay, direct.txdelay, CR) and proposes a density metric to automatically adjust them to optimize a repeater for its current environment. By automating this, the entire mesh would improve (collisions would be reduced) and repeater owners that leave their repeaters at the non-optimum default values would have them automatically adjusted to an improved value. Comments for improvements and corrections would be greatly appreciated.
messages.content_html: Attached is a document that examines the various repeater parameters (rxdelay, txdelay, direct.txdelay, CR) and proposes a density metric to automatically adjust them to optimize a repeater for its current environment. By automating this, the entire mesh would improve (collisions would be reduced) and repeater owners that leave their repeaters at the non-optimum default values would have them automatically adjusted to an improved value. Comments for improvements and corrections would be greatly appreciated.
messages.reactions_html: 7

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: We manually set our repeaters with the values, however the true benefit is achieved when the neighbor repeaters use them. Setting our repeaters benefitted our neighbors, by spreading our transmissions and reducing or eliminating a collision from us. We only used the values with two local repeaters, but with several other repeaters around, which did not use them, we could not make a definitive assessment. (edited)
messages.content_html: We manually set our repeaters with the values, however the true benefit is achieved when the neighbor repeaters use them. Setting our repeaters benefitted our neighbors, by spreading our transmissions and reducing or eliminating a collision from us. We only used the values with two local repeaters, but with several other repeaters around, which did not use them, we could not make a definitive assessment. (edited)

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Well I would choose a dense mesh (this should be having collisions). Baseline the stats of the repeaters (the ones of interest are the uptime, TX/RX packets and error packets) of each and record the current settings. Manually count the neighbors and find the setting and set them via the CLI. Let it run for a week and recollect the stats. Hopefully the error rate has gone down. I’m not sure how to measure if the Delivered / Failed DM rate changes (are ACKs getting through better).
messages.content_html: Well I would choose a dense mesh (this should be having collisions). Baseline the stats of the repeaters (the ones of interest are the uptime, TX/RX packets and error packets) of each and record the current settings. Manually count the neighbors and find the setting and set them via the CLI. Let it run for a week and recollect the stats. Hopefully the error rate has gone down. I’m not sure how to measure if the Delivered / Failed DM rate changes (are ACKs getting through better).

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Then set the values back to the original run for a week and compare old vs. new RX rate, TX rate, Error rate.
messages.content_html: Then set the values back to the original run for a week and compare old vs. new RX rate, TX rate, Error rate.

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Section 7, CR is mostly an overview on how it works, but does have the four paragraphs for the 4 density environments. The “beef” is in section 9.4.2/2 which added a CR column and the algorithm is used to choose the row. There is a note regarding Sparse, it may be easier to just go one notch higher, rather than tweaking the counting to include low SNR neighbors (it’s not much of a hit to airtime) and simplifies how it’s measured.
messages.content_html: Section 7, CR is mostly an overview on how it works, but does have the four paragraphs for the 4 density environments. The “beef” is in section 9.4.2/2 which added a CR column and the algorithm is used to choose the row. There is a note regarding Sparse, it may be easier to just go one notch higher, rather than tweaking the counting to include low SNR neighbors (it’s not much of a hit to airtime) and simplifies how it’s measured.

messages.author: paul / BostonMesh
messages.text: Thanks! We are still working on updating the delay & cr values on repeaters, but starting to see different behavior on our live map. Also looking to setup an overlay so that you can see the type of node a repeater is (sparse/medium/dense/regional/etc). That will be manual, but still potentially helpful when watching the live traffic.

Also looking to setup an overlay so that you can see the type of node a repeater is (sparse/medium/dense/regional/etc). That will be manual, but still potentially helpful when watching the live traffic.

messages.author: paul / BostonMesh
messages.text: 1.14 has identified that it is non-trivial to get everyone to update all of the repeaters. While I’m absolutely in favor of adding this as a dynamic function within the firmware, it’s going to take awhile before it sees adoption. More so with an entirely different fork of the firmware.

While I’m absolutely in favor of adding this as a dynamic function within the firmware, it’s going to take awhile before it sees adoption. More so with an entirely different fork of the firmware.

messages.text: ZephCore is beta, I'm not suggesting switching to it, I was asking about his approach to the same problem

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: I totally agree, but I believe the easiest way to mass adoption is to just put it in the code. If they update, they get the automatic optimization benefit, if they don't it's the same as what we have today. Over time we will catch everyone. I also agree that some sort of testing could be done, but as you noted, it's not easy to get a group of people to participate and to really validate this it needs to be tried on the various "mesh environments". Maybe just a theoretical assessment is all that is required. I believe the new settings can only improve the current situation (given that the defaults are way too low). If you have "any results" with your static manual setting please let me know, I would like to see what happened. I've been thinking I might need to buy a nice waterfall frequency analyzer, with storage, to capture what is happening at my roof, I suspect that collisions are occurring, but I'm not sure what type of instrument can capture the RF coming in and identify what is going on...

messages.author: Aleksey M.
messages.text: A not very expensive SDR receiver with a good receiving path (bandpass filter) is enough to see collisions at the waterfall in a fairly dense network. I see them all the time. In addition, I think that on the attached SNR graph of all messages received by my companion, this "hanging down fringe" is actually collisions that sharply reduce the SNR of received packets. There is not much other interference in our band. So I am very glad to see your autotuning proposal, thank you very much! Unfortunately, I'm sure that I won't be able to get a significant number of neighboring repeaters to update to the test build, so I'm looking forward to the official release of this amazing feature. (edited)

I also agree that some sort of testing could be done, but as you noted, it's not easy to get a group of people to participate and to really validate this it needs to be tried on the various "mesh environments". Maybe just a theoretical assessment is all that is required. I believe the new settings can only improve the current situation (given that the defaults are way too low). If you have "any results" with your static manual setting please let me know, I would like to see what happened. I've been thinking I might need to buy a nice waterfall frequency analyzer, with storage, to capture what is happening at my roof, I suspect that collisions are occurring, but I'm not sure what type of instrument can capture the RF coming in and identify what is going on...

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Who makes the SDR you have? I presume a good Cavity Filter in between the antenna and SDR would work well to keep out any surrounding noise. It also looks like there is MeshCore software that is decoding the ID of the messages that it’s receiving, where do it get that? Thanks! for the tip…

A not very expensive SDR receiver with a good receiving path (bandpass filter) is enough to see collisions at the waterfall in a fairly dense network. I see them all the time. In addition, I think that on the attached SNR graph of all messages received by my companion, this "hanging down fringe" is actually collisions that sharply reduce the SNR of received packets. There is not much other interference in our band. So I am very glad to see your autotuning proposal, thank you very much! Unfortunately, I'm sure that I won't be able to get a significant number of neighboring repeaters to update to the test build, so I'm looking forward to the official release of this amazing feature. (edited)

messages.author: Aleksey M.
messages.text: Nothing special, just SDRplay RSPdx R2 with an inexpensive Chinese SAW-filter. And I collect statistics on received messages using a USB companion and HomeAssistant with meshcore integration and automation that creates entities for the last prefixes from the packet path, as well as placing the corresponding SNR and RSSI in them. This data is accumulated with InfluxDB and visualized using Grafana. This looks quite complicated to someone unfamiliar with HomeAssistant and related solutions, but it allows you to get a useful result in a shorter time compared to developing a special application. Here is the link to the HomeAssistant integration site https://meshcore-dev.github.io/meshcore-ha/ And a link to a solution that saves the SNR of packets received from repeaters https://github.com/WJ4IoT/Meshcore-Home-Assistant-Solutions/blob/main/SNR_Repeater.md (edited)
messages.content_html: Nothing special, just SDRplay RSPdx R2 with an inexpensive Chinese SAW-filter. And I collect statistics on received messages using a USB companion and HomeAssistant with meshcore integration and automation that creates entities for the last prefixes from the packet path, as well as placing the corresponding SNR and RSSI in them. This data is accumulated with InfluxDB and visualized using Grafana. This looks quite complicated to someone unfamiliar with HomeAssistant and related solutions, but it allows you to get a useful result in a shorter time compared to developing a special application. Here is the link to the HomeAssistant integration site https://meshcore-dev.github.io/meshcore-ha/ And a link to a solution that saves the SNR of packets received from repeaters https://github.com/WJ4IoT/Meshcore-Home-Assistant-Solutions/blob/main/SNR_Repeater.md (edited)
